content:
Key permissions, authorized_keys, and the ssh-agent gotchas that waste an hour

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Permission denied (publickey) means the SSH server received your connection but rejected every key you offered. Password auth is usually disabled on production servers. The fix is on the client side (wrong key, ssh-agent not loaded) or server side (key not in authorized_keys, wrong file permissions, wrong user).
This error ate an hour of my life last month because I was using the right key file but ssh-agent had a different key loaded, and -v output buried the mismatch under forty lines of debug noise.
What the Error Actually Means
When you run ssh user@host, the client offers public keys in this order:

• Keys currently loaded in ssh-agent

• Keys listed in ~/.ssh/config for that host

• Default keys: ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, etc.
The server checks each offered key against ~/.ssh/authorized_keys on the remote machine. If none match, you get Permission denied (publickey). The server never tells you which key it expected — only that yours didn't work.
Quick Answer
Run verbose mode immediately:
ssh -v user@host 2>&1 | grep -E "Offering|Authentications|Permission"Look for Offering public key lines. If your expected key file never appears in "Offering," the client isn't sending it. If it appears but auth still fails, the server doesn't have that public key in authorized_keys, or permissions on the server are wrong.
Force a specific key to isolate the problem:
ssh -i ~/.ssh/my_deploy_key user@hostCommon Causes

• Wrong key file — you generated a new key for this server but ssh is offering a default key instead.

• ssh-agent has a different key loaded — agent offers key A; server expects key B. Your explicit -i flag can even be overridden depending on IdentitiesOnly settings.

• Public key not in server’s authorized_keys — key was never copied, copied to wrong user, or pasted with a line break in the middle.

• Wrong username — key is in deploy@host authorized_keys but you're connecting as ubuntu@host.

• Server-side permission errors — ~/.ssh must be 700, authorized_keys must be 600, home directory must not be group-writable. SSH refuses keys if permissions are too open.

• SELinux or filesystem context — common on RHEL/CentOS after manual file edits. restorecon -R -v ~/.ssh fixes it.

• sshd_config disables pubkey auth — PubkeyAuthentication no or AuthorizedKeysFile pointing somewhere unexpected.

• Key type rejected — old server doesn’t accept ed25519; or server disabled RSA and you only have id_rsa.
Step-by-Step Fix
1. Confirm which keys your client is offering
ssh -vv user@host 2>&1 | grep "Offering public key"Note every key offered. Compare the key comment (usually email or filename at the end) to what you expect.
List what’s in your agent:
ssh-add -lIf the right key isn’t listed, either add it or bypass the agent entirely (step 2).
2. Force the correct identity file
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_prod -o IdentitiesOnly=yes user@hostIdentitiesOnly=yes stops ssh-agent from offering other keys that might confuse debugging. If this works, your fix is permanent via ~/.ssh/config:
Host prod-server
HostName 10.0.1.50
User deploy
IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_prod
IdentitiesOnly yes3. Fix local private key permissions
SSH refuses to use a private key that’s readable by others:
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_prod
chmod 700 ~/.sshIf you see WARNING: UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE!, this is the cause. Fix permissions and retry.
4. Verify the public key matches what the server has
Print your local public key:
ssh-keygen -y -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_prodCompare character-for-character to what’s on the server. If you have console access (cloud provider serial console, rescue mode, another user who can SSH in), check the server:
cat ~/.ssh/authorized_keysOne key per line. No line breaks in the middle of a key. The key type prefix (ssh-ed25519, ssh-rsa) must match.
5. Install your public key on the server
If you have password auth temporarily enabled:
ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_prod.pub user@hostManual method (via console or another working account):
mkdir -p ~/.ssh
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
echo "ssh-ed25519 AAAA...your-key... comment" >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys6. Fix server-side permissions
On the server (via console if SSH is completely broken):
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
chmod go-w ~SSH is strict: if ~ is group-writable or world-writable, pubkey auth is disabled even with correct keys. Check with:
ls -la ~ | head -5
ls -la ~/.ssh/Home should be drwx------ or drwxr-xr-x (not group-writable). .ssh must be 700. authorized_keys must be 600.
7. Check sshd configuration on the server
sudo grep -E "PubkeyAuthentication|AuthorizedKeysFile|PasswordAuthentication" /etc/ssh/sshd_configYou want PubkeyAuthentication yes. Note the AuthorizedKeysFile path — keys must be in that file, not a different location. After any change:
sudo systemctl restart sshd
# or on older systems:
sudo service ssh restartBe careful: test with a second session before closing your working connection. Locking yourself out during sshd restart is a classic mistake.
8. Resolve ssh-agent gotchas
If ssh-add -l shows keys but the wrong one authenticates first:
ssh-add -D # clear all agent keys
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_prod # add only the one you needOn macOS, keys loaded into the Keychain can auto-offer even when you don’t want them. IdentitiesOnly yes in config is your friend.
For CI/CD pipelines: most failures are $KEY env var with trailing newline, or the key written to a temp file without chmod 600. Always:
echo "$SSH_PRIVATE_KEY" | tr -d '\r' > ~/.ssh/deploy_key
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/deploy_keyVerify the Fix
ssh -o BatchMode=yes -o ConnectTimeout=5 user@host echo okBatchMode=yes disables password prompts — exit 0 with ok printed means pubkey auth works non-interactively. That's what your deploy scripts need.
Double-check from a clean environment (no agent keys loaded):
ssh-add -D
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_prod -o IdentitiesOnly=yes user@hostIf that works, your config fix is solid.
Prevent It From Happening Again
→ One host block per server in ~/.ssh/config. Explicit IdentityFile and IdentitiesOnly yes eliminates agent confusion. → Document which key goes to which environment. A README in the team's infra repo beats guessing between id_rsa_prod and id_rsa_staging. → Automate key distribution. Ansible authorized_key module, Terraform aws_key_pair, cloud-init ssh_authorized_keys — manual ssh-copy-id doesn't scale and leaves stragglers. → Audit server permissions after manual edits. Any time someone hand-edits authorized_keys, run the chmod checklist. → Test CI SSH in a dry-run job. A pipeline step that runs ssh -o BatchMode=yes before the real deploy catches key format and permission issues early.
Bastion and Jump Host Chains
If you connect through a bastion (ssh -J bastion user@internal), pubkey auth must work at each hop. A common failure: your key is in internal authorized_keys but not bastion, or the bastion's ssh-agent forwarding isn't enabled.
ssh -J bastion.example.com -i ~/.ssh/deploy_key -o IdentitiesOnly=yes deploy@10.0.1.50For ProxyJump in config:
Host internal-app
HostName 10.0.1.50
User deploy
ProxyJump bastion.example.com
IdentityFile ~/.ssh/deploy_key
IdentitiesOnly yesTest each hop independently: ssh bastion first, then ssh internal from the bastion. Isolate which hop rejects the key.
When It’s Not a Key Problem
Connection refused — sshd isn't running or wrong port. Connection timed out — security group, firewall, or wrong IP. Host key verification failed — server's host key changed (or MITM; investigate before overriding).
Too many authentication failures — ssh offered too many keys and the server dropped the connection before the right one. Fix with IdentitiesOnly yes or ssh -o IdentitiesOnly=yes.
Permission denied (publickey) is almost always a mismatch between what your client offers and what the server accepts. Verbose mode, force the right key with IdentitiesOnly, fix permissions on both sides. Twenty minutes if you read the Offering lines first; an hour if you don't.
